The heartbeat is steady.
You write one word on the form.
Unremarkable.
The room is clean. The light works. The gloves are new.
Same equipment you'd find in any clinic anywhere.
Same fluorescent light overhead.
Different room.
Different purpose.
You don't think about the difference anymore.
Or you do, and it doesn't slow you down.
Two hands.
Two rooms.
The hands don't change.
Only what they're used for.
You didn't plan this. Nobody plans this.
You were trained to diagnose, to observe, to report.
The training works in both rooms.
That's the problem.
You are twenty-nine.
Medical school cost more than your parents' house.
Seven years of training, and the debt still grows faster than you can pay it.
Your hospital salary covers the rent, the electricity, the insurance.
Not the interest.
You eat lunch at the hospital cafeteria because it's three dollars cheaper.
You don't remember when that started.
You take the bus home instead of driving, most weeks.
You studied medicine to help people.
You're good at it. You know you're good at it.
But good doesn't pay the interest.
That part hasn't started yet.
A man calls your office line on a Thursday.
He calls you Doctor.
Not your name. Just Doctor.
He has a simple request.
Blood compatibility test. Tissue typing.
Standard workup for a standard patient.
He'll send the patient to you.
You'll run the labs, write the results.
He'll pick up the report.
No one else needs to see it.
He says it the way a cardiologist would refer a case to a nephrologist.
Clean, professional, routine.
He mentions a number.
More than your monthly salary, for one exam.
You don't say yes.
You don't say no either.
You write down the address.
The address is a back office behind a pharmacy.
End of an alley, past a metal gate that doesn't lock.
The light is yellow and hums when you flip it on.
The equipment is old, but it works.
Stethoscope. Blood pressure cuff. Sample tubes.
A sharps container that's never been emptied.
Everything you'd find in a real clinic.
Because it is one.
Just not yours.
The first one sits across from you.
Thin. Dark circles under the eyes.
He doesn't look at you. He looks at the floor.
You place the stethoscope on his chest, the way you've done it a thousand times before.
But your hand isn't steady.
You check the blood pressure. You check it again.
You listen to the heart. You listen to it again.
Not because the readings were unclear.
You write the results on a form with no letterhead.
No hospital logo. No department name. Just your handwriting.
You sign at the bottom.
The pen feels heavier than it should.
You drive home with the radio off.
The streetlights pass in the windshield one at a time.
The envelope is in the glove compartment.
You don't open it until the car is parked, engine off, windows up.
Three months of hospital overtime, in a single envelope.
For one exam. One patient. One form.
One signature.
You tell yourself it was just a blood test.
Just numbers on a page.
You've run a hundred of them.
This one just went to a different address.
You wash your hands before dinner.
You wash them again before bed.
You wash them a third time and stand there looking at the faucet.
The soap smells the same as it always does.
Your hands smell different.
Or you think they do.
Morning.
Eleven o'clock, second patient of the day.
A woman sits on your exam table. Routine checkup.
You listen to her heart.
You take her blood pressure. Normal.
You write: within normal range.
She smiles and says thank you, Doctor.
You nod.
That part still feels normal.
That evening.
Seven-thirty, back office behind the pharmacy.
A man sits on a different table.
You listen to his heart, the same way.
You take his blood pressure. Normal.
You write: within normal range.
Same words. Same handwriting. Same pen.
He doesn't smile.
He doesn't say anything.
You use the same sentence you used this morning.
One patient says thank you. The other says nothing.
You stop checking twice.
You used to listen to the heart, then listen again, just to be sure.
Now you listen once.
Then you stop listening that long.
The stethoscope doesn't care how long you hold it there.
It picks up whatever's there. That's enough.
Your ears still hear the same thing, either way.
Some nights you drive home and try to separate the morning exams from the evening ones in your head.
Morning patients and evening donors, all in the same list.
You try to put them in different categories.
Then you stop trying.
The sample goes through the hospital lab.
You write a name on the tube that doesn't belong to anyone.
It sits on the rack between the ten o'clock cholesterol panel and the eleven o'clock thyroid check.
Nobody looks at the name on the tube.
The machine reads the barcode. The machine reads the numbers.
Your handwriting on the false name is steady now.
It used to shake.
It doesn't.
A donor looks at you during the exam.
Not at the equipment. Not at the wall.
At you.
He asks: is this safe.
You answer the way you've practiced.
Same tone you use in the morning clinic.
Same pause before the reassurance.
Everything is within normal range. No abnormalities detected.
He nods slowly.
He believes you because you sound like a doctor.
You sound like a doctor because you are one.

Suitable for procedure.
Your pen hovers over the box.
Not because you don't know the answer.
Because you do.
You know what the checkbox means.
You know where the form goes after you sign it.
You know what happens to the person sitting across from you after you check it.
You check the box.
You sign.
The next one comes two weeks later. You don't hesitate.
The one after that comes ten days later. You don't look up.
The one after that comes a week later.
You adjust the stethoscope around your neck. Habit.
There's a water stain on the ceiling tile above the exam chair.
You notice it every time.

Smaller volume than usual.

You hold it up to the light. The blood sits at the bottom, barely covering the line.
You don't ask why the volume is small.
You already know why, and you don't want to hear it confirmed.
You run the test anyway.
You label the tube, the same way you label every tube.
The false name doesn't bother you anymore. The small volume does.
The results come back clean.
Everything within normal range.
Healthy. Viable. Compatible.

Nobody sees it.

One week later.
The broker calls again.
A different doctor cleared the sample.
Someone less careful.
Or not less careful. Just less willing to stop.

It didn't matter that you said no.
It mattered that someone said yes.

It needs a signature.

You don't look at the volume.

Blood type. Kidney function. Tissue match.
Compatibility.

The name was never the point.
You're not sure when you stopped looking at it.
Morning shift.

Evening.

The training doesn't know the difference between a patient and a donor.
